title: Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps
text: Shiny is an R package that lets you build interactive web applications directly from R code — no need to know HTML, CSS, or JavaScript. If you can write R, you can build a working web app. This is especially useful for anyone doing data analysis who wants to let others explore that data themselves, rather than just handing over a static report.

title: Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps
text: Shiny is an R package that lets you build interactive web applications directly from R code — no need to know HTML, CSS, or JavaScript. If you can write R, you can build a working web app. This is especially useful for anyone doing data analysis who wants to let others explore that data themselves, rather than just handing over a static report.

title: Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps
section: The Two Core Pieces: UI and Server

title: Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps
text:
Reactivity: The Heart of Shiny
What makes Shiny powerful is reactivity — when a user changes an input (like moving a slider), Shiny automatically re-runs only the parts of the code that depend on that input, and updates the output in real time. You don’t have to manually refresh anything; it just works.

title: Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps
text:
Why This Matters
Shiny apps are ideal when you want to:

Let non-technical stakeholders explore data themselves
Build internal tools without needing a full web development team
Turn a one-time analysis into something reusable and interactive

title: Essay: Understanding Shiny Apps
text:
Requirement: A Running Server
One important thing to understand about traditional Shiny apps: they need to run on a server. That means someone (you, your IT team, or a hosting service like shinyapps.io) has to keep an R process running in the background to power the app. This is important context for understanding Shinylive, covered in the next essay, which solves this exact limitation.

title: Essay: Understanding Shinylive
text: Shinylive is a way to run Shiny apps entirely in a web browser, without needing a dedicated R server running in the background. It works by compiling R (or Python) into WebAssembly, a technology that lets code run directly inside the browser itself. In simple terms: the “server” logic that used to live on a remote machine now runs right on the visitor’s own computer, inside their browser tab.

title: Essay: Understanding Shinylive
text: Shinylive is a way to run Shiny apps entirely in a web browser, without needing a dedicated R server running in the background. It works by compiling R (or Python) into WebAssembly, a technology that lets code run directly inside the browser itself. In simple terms: the “server” logic that used to live on a remote machine now runs right on the visitor’s own computer, inside their browser tab.

title: Essay: Understanding Shinylive
section: How It Differs From Traditional Shiny
text:
How It Differs From Traditional Shiny









Traditional Shiny
Shinylive




Where it runs
On a server (needs to stay running)
In the user’s browser (no server needed)


Hosting
Requires a server or service like shinyapps.io
Can be hosted as static files (e.g., GitHub Pages)


Cost
Ongoing server costs
Free to host as static files


Startup time
Usually fast
Slower first load (browser downloads R/Python runtime)


Package support
Full access to any R package
Limited to packages compatible with WebAssembly


Scalability
Server can get overwhelmed with many users
Scales naturally since each user runs their own copy

title: Essay: Understanding Shinylive
text:
Pros of Shinylive

No server required — eliminates hosting costs and maintenance
Easy to share — can be hosted anywhere static files are supported, including GitHub Pages
Scales effortlessly — since each visitor’s browser does the work, there’s no risk of the app slowing down under heavy traffic

title: Essay: Understanding Shinylive
text:
Cons of Shinylive

Slower initial load — the browser has to download the R (or Python) runtime environment before the app starts
Limited package support — not every R package works in this browser-based environment yet
Less suited for heavy computation — since it runs on the visitor’s device, very data-intensive apps may perform worse than on a dedicated server

title: Essay: Understanding Shinylive
text:
When to Use Each
Traditional Shiny is still the better choice for apps that need heavy computation, access to a database, or use packages not yet supported in WebAssembly. Shinylive shines for lightweight, shareable tools you want to distribute freely without worrying about hosting infrastructure — a perfect fit for a public-facing demo or a free educational tool.
